title: Writing - Part 1: Plastic Pollution
description: Your class has been discussing how to tackle the serious environmental problem of plastic pollution.

content.question: Write an essay discussing two of the solutions in your notes. You should explain which solution you think would be more effective, giving reasons for your opinion.

opinions: Single-use plastics such as straws and bags are unnecessary and should be banned entirely.
Better recycling infrastructure would stop plastics from ending up in oceans and landfills.
Teaching children about environmental responsibility creates lasting behavioural change.

structure.intro: Introduce the plastic pollution crisis and state which two solutions you will discuss.
structure.body1: Discuss the first solution with evidence of its potential effectiveness.
structure.body2: Discuss the second solution and explain which you consider more effective.
structure.conclusion: Summarise and give your overall recommendation.

content.modelAnswer:
Plastic pollution has reached alarming levels, threatening marine life and contaminating food chains worldwide. Two potential solutions are reducing the production of single-use plastics and improving recycling systems.

Banning or heavily taxing single-use plastics addresses the problem at its source. Products such as disposable cups, bags and packaging constitute a huge proportion of plastic waste. Several countries have already introduced such measures with notable success, showing that when fewer single-use plastics are produced, environmental impact decreases.

Improving recycling systems is another crucial step. In many regions, recycling facilities are underfunded and inefficient, meaning a significant portion of recyclable material ends up in landfill or the ocean. Investing in better infrastructure and making recycling more convenient for households would help prevent plastic from polluting the environment.

In my opinion, reducing the production of single-use plastics is the more effective solution. If fewer plastics are manufactured in the first place, there is less to recycle or dispose of. Targeting production prevents the problem from arising, rather than simply managing its consequences. However, both measures are needed for a truly comprehensive response to plastic pollution.
